title: Ancient DNA Switches Found in Plants
summary: Scientists have discovered ancient DNA switches in plants that are over 400 million years old. These switches, called conserved non-coding sequences (CNSs), help control when genes turn on or off in plants. Researchers used a new tool called Conservatory to find more than 2.3 million of these switches by comparing the DNA of 314 plant genomes from 284 species. They found that some switches are very old, dating back to before flowering plants existed. The study shows that these switches are important for plant growth and development. Scientists can now use this information to learn more about how plants evolve and to help improve crops like food plants. This discovery helps solve a long-standing puzzle about whether such DNA switches stay the same in plants over millions of years.

questions.question: Why is this discovery useful for crops?

background_read: Plants have DNA that controls how they grow and develop. Scientists study this DNA to understand how plants have changed over millions of years. DNA switches are special parts that tell genes when to work. Finding ancient switches helps us learn about plant history and can make crops better for farming.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory and other labs
WHAT: Discovered ancient DNA switches in plants that control genes
WHERE: In plant genomes from around the world
WHY: To solve a puzzle about plant evolution and help improve crops

perspectives.description: This finding shows that DNA switches in plants have been conserved for millions of years, helping us understand plant biology better.
